outlook.summary: Not financial advice — this is a read of the public data and news above, meant to help you decide, not a recommendation. Always size positions and confirm with your own research.

strongest.reason: The global chip rally extended for a second session — Korea's Kospi jumped 4.6%, Nvidia climbed after taking a stake in neocloud provider Nebius, Taiwan Semiconductor rose over 5% on continued post-earnings momentum, and Arm rallied further after Jefferies raised its price target — all ahead of AMD's 'Advancing AI 2026' event and a wave of chip-adjacent earnings this week.

Cybersecurity names like Palo Alto Networks (+92% YTD), CrowdStrike (+74% YTD) and Fortinet (~+100% YTD) continue holding up even as parts of the broader AI trade wobble, with global cybersecurity spending forecast to top $300 billion in 2026.

weakest.reason: Tesla heads into today's Q2 print with Optimus still unavailable to the public and a new competitive threat from OpenAI's newly announced robotics division — feeding the same investor debate about whether robotaxi and Optimus progress can justify the AI investment cycle.

ServiceNow's Q2 report today lands after IBM's warning that clients are shifting budget away from general software spend toward security and infrastructure, and shares are down sharply year-to-date; Salesforce and Adobe are both trading mixed to lower into the same backdrop.

outlook.nextStep: Alphabet's and Tesla's earnings today are the more immediate, concrete catalysts worth a closer look, alongside ServiceNow's print after the close given the IBM-driven budget-shift questions hanging over enterprise software; the Fed's hawkish tilt under new Chair Kevin Warsh and the Iran-driven oil spike are still fluid and better watched than acted on until there's more clarity.

signals.title: Fed's hawkish tilt collides with a still-elevated oil shock
signals.body: New Fed Chair Kevin Warsh held rates at 3.50%-3.75% but shortened the policy statement, dropped forward guidance and formed task forces to review Fed process; roughly half of FOMC members reportedly indicated openness to a hike later this year if inflation doesn't cooperate. Separately, Brent crude is holding near $89/bbl and WTI near $83, up as US-Iran hostilities continue to threaten shipping through the Strait of Hormuz.
signals.watch: Upcoming inflation prints, further Fed commentary on hike odds, and whether shipping disruption in the Strait of Hormuz spreads.
signals.focus: Lower conviction right now — two fast-moving, partly overlapping macro risks with wide potential outcomes in either direction.

signals.title: Chip stocks extend their rebound as the global AI trade rallies again
signals.body: Asia-Pacific chip bellwethers led a broad rally (Kospi +4.6%), and Nvidia, Taiwan Semiconductor and Arm all extended gains — Nvidia after taking a stake in neocloud provider Nebius, TSM on continued post-earnings momentum, and Arm after Jefferies lifted its price target. AMD's 'Advancing AI 2026' event this week (Zen 6 Venice CPU, MI400-series roadmap) is the next catalyst.
signals.watch: Whether AMD's event and this week's other chip-adjacent earnings sustain the rebound or reignite the capex-fatigue concerns from earlier in July.
signals.focus: Worth a closer look — multiple concrete, company-specific catalysts are lining up in the same direction rather than just broad sentiment.

signals.title: Alphabet's Q2 call today is the swing event for the AI-infrastructure trade
signals.body: Alphabet reports after the close today, with options pricing in a roughly 5.4% move. Focus areas include cloud profitability, 2027 capex guidance, and the rollout timeline for Gemini; Alphabet's stake in Anthropic has also been marked up sharply since March, which some analysts think could push reported EPS well above consensus.
signals.watch: Management's direct commentary on the Gemini timeline and capex guidance for 2027, and how peers react into their own earnings later this week.
signals.focus: Worth a closer look — this is likely the single biggest near-term catalyst for the AI-infrastructure group.

signals.title: Tesla's earnings put the Optimus timeline under the microscope, with OpenAI now a rival
signals.body: Tesla reports Q2 results today with Optimus production still not public-facing; a converted Fremont line is reportedly targeting production between late July and August. Over the weekend, OpenAI announced a new dedicated robotics division, adding a fresh competitive question to the humanoid-robot narrative just as Tesla shares trade lower into the print.
signals.watch: Any specific Optimus production timeline Tesla gives today, and how OpenAI's robotics push is received by the market.
signals.focus: Lower conviction right now — the gap between the physical-AI narrative and near-term production reality is worth watching closely before drawing conclusions.

signals.title: ServiceNow's Q2 test lands after IBM's enterprise-software budget warning
signals.body: ServiceNow reports Q2 results today after the close, with consensus looking for roughly 22% year-over-year revenue growth driven by AI-native offerings like Now Assist. The print comes shortly after IBM warned that some clients are shifting budget away from general software spend, a dynamic that has weighed on ServiceNow, Salesforce and Adobe shares this year.
signals.watch: Whether ServiceNow's results and guidance confirm or push back on the IBM-driven budget-reallocation story.
signals.focus: Worth a closer look — this print is a read on enterprise AI software demand more broadly, not just one company.

signals.title: Bitcoin and Ether ETFs post a fifth straight day of inflows as risk appetite returns
signals.body: US spot Bitcoin ETFs pulled in $227 million and spot Ether ETFs added $38 million, both marking a fifth consecutive day of net inflows. Futures open interest is up over 9% and options volume has more than doubled, even as the broader macro backdrop stays cautious with the US-Iran standoff ongoing.
signals.watch: Whether the inflow streak extends to a sixth day, which would strengthen the case that institutional demand is decoupling from the geopolitical risk-off mood.
signals.focus: Worth a closer look — a five-day inflow streak is a more concrete data point than day-to-day price swings.

signals.title: Cybersecurity keeps holding up even as parts of the AI trade wobble
signals.body: Cybersecurity names such as Palo Alto Networks, CrowdStrike and Fortinet are holding near highs even in sessions where broader AI and semiconductor names sell off, a divergence tied to resilient security budgets even as general software spend faces scrutiny. Global cybersecurity spending is forecast to exceed $300 billion in 2026.
signals.watch: Whether upcoming earnings from cybersecurity names confirm continued budget resilience, and whether the sector's premium valuations hold if the broader market rotates.
signals.focus: Worth checking in on, but keep in mind some of this strength may already be priced into current valuations.
